Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## MIND YOUR SOLVER! ON ADVERSARIAL ATTACK AND DEFENSE FOR COMBINATORIAL OPTIMIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Combinatorial optimization (CO) is a long-standing challenging task not only in its inherent complexity (e.g. NP-hard) but also the possible sensitivity to input conditions. In this paper, we take an initiative on developing the mechanisms for adversarial attack and defense towards combinatorial optimization solvers, whereby the solver is treated as a black-box function and the original problem’s underlying graph structure (which is often available and associated with the problem instance, e.g. DAG, TSP) is attacked under a given budget. Experimental results on three real-world combinatorial optimization problems reveal the vulnerability of existing solvers to adversarial attack, including the commercial solvers like Gurobi. In particular, we present a simple yet effective defense strategy to modify the graph structure to increase the robustness of solvers, which shows its universal effectiveness across tasks and solvers.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The combinatorial optimization (CO) problems are widely studied due to their importance in practice (e.g. job scheduling, routing, matching, etc). In the last century, a variety of heuristic methods (Van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987; Whitley, 1994) are proposed to tackle these standing and often NP-hard problems. Driven by the recent development of deep learning and reinforcement learning, many learning-based methods (Khalil et al., 2017; Mao et al., 2019; Kwon et al., 2021) are also developed in this area, which show promising potential often for their cost-efficiency.

Despite the success of solvers in various combinatorial optimization tasks, few attention has been paid to the vulnerability and robustness of combinatorial solvers, regardless of whether they are learning based or not. A line of relevant works aims at handling combinatorial optimization under uncertainty (Buchheim & Kurtz, 2018). However, to our best knowledge, ensuring the robustness of combinatorial solvers with slightly modified problem instances remains relatively unexplored. It is worth noting that many CO problems can be essentially formulated as a graph problem (Khalil et al., 2017; Bengio et al., 2020), hence it is attractive and natural to modify the problem instance by modifying the graph structure, to generate more test cases for solvers. In fact, vulnerability can often be an inherent challenge for CO solvers since the problem is often strong nonlinear and NP-hard. From this perspective, we consider attack and defense CO solvers in the following aspects.

From the attack side, developing attack models can be useful for thoroughly evaluating a solver’s robustness. The solvers may be more fragile than the general impression: for traditional learning-free solvers, in some cases, their heuristics and hyperparameters may not be universal and stable enough such that a small change on problem condition or graph structure may deteriorate the performance notably. This also holds for recent machine learning based solvers as the model may be overfit and the objective landscape can be complex due to the inherent difficulty of discrete CO problems.

As a result, it is imperative to develop defense mechanisms and techniques to improve the robustness of CO solvers, either for learning-based models or traditional ones, especially if the approach can be in black-box mode without knowing the details of the solvers. In particular, it is even desirable to develop out-of-box defense mechanism. Our hope is that this may be realized when the problem instance change[1] involves only graph structure variation – which is often the case.

1Readers may argue that there are little deliberate attacks to CO solvers, while one can regard such attacks as the problem instance variation which can often happen in real-world e.g. when the network takes a small daily change in Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) and Fraud Coverage problems as will be studied in our experiments.

To this end, we present **Ro** bust **C** ombnaotorial **O** ptimization (ROCO), a framework for testing and improving the robustness of a given combinatorial optimization solver. Table 1 compares our framework to classical works in images and graphs. Our attacker limits the number of attacked edges in the graph and guarantees that the optimal solution must not become worse. Our defender ensures that the new solution is also feasible for the pre-defended problem. The overview of ROCO framework is summarized in Fig. 1. **In summary, this paper makes the following contributions** :

**1)** Given the fact that combinatorial problems can often be represented by underlying graphs, we propose to perform adversarial attacks toward CO solvers to deteriorate their solution quality. To our best knowledge, this is the pioneering work that formally studies adversarial attacks on combinatorial solvers, though their vulnerability has been occasionally recognized by the community.

**2)** We propose ROCO, an adversarial framework that consists of both attack and defense models on top of CO solvers. We design our attack models with both learning-based and traditional simulated annealing methods by slightly modifying the graph structures (e.g. add, delete or modify edges). To increase the robustness of the combinatorial solvers, we further propose defense mechanism against attacks. Our attack and defense models are applicable to solvers regardless of learning-based or not.

**3)** We implement and apply our adversarial attack and defense models to three common combinatorial optimization tasks: Directed Acyclic Graph Scheduling, Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem and Fraud Coverage. The experimental results on black-box attack/defense show the effectiveness and generality of our approach. The source code will be made public available.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Combinatorial optimization.** As a widely studied problem, there exist many traditional algorithms for CO, including but not limited to greedy algorithms, heuristic algorithms like simulated annealing (SA) (Van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987) or Lin–Kernighan–Helsgaun (LKH3) (Helsgaun, 2017), as well as commercial solvers like Gurobi (Gurobi Optimization, 2020). Besides, driven by the recent development of deep learning and reinforce learning, many learning-based methods have also been proposed to tackle these problems. A mainstream approach using deep learning is to predict the solution end-to-end, such as the supervised model Pointer Networks (Vinyals et al., 2015), reinforcement learning models S2V-DQN (Khalil et al., 2017) and MatNet (Kwon et al., 2021). Though these methods did perform well on different types of COPs, they are not that robust and universal, as discussed in (Bengio et al., 2020), the solvers may get stuck around poor solutions in many cases. Different from works (Moon et al., 2019; Zang et al., 2020) which apply CO for attack against neural networks, we take an initiative on the adversarial attack and defense on CO.

**Adversarial attack and defense.** Since the seminal study (Szegedy et al., 2014) showed that small input perturbations can change model predictions, many adversarial attack methods have been devised to construct such attacks. In general, adversarial attacks can be roughly divided into two categories: white-box attacks with access to the model gradients, e.g. (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Madry et al., 2018; Carlini & Wagner, 2017), and black-box attacks, with only access to the model predictions, e.g. (Ilyas et al., 2018; Narodytska & Kasiviswanathan, 2016). Besides image and text adversarial attacks (Jia & Liang, 2017), given the importance of graph-related applications and the successful applications of graph neural networks (GNN) (Scarselli et al., 2008), more attentions are recently paid to the robustness of GNNs. In the mean time, many defense strategies like adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016; Tram`er et al., 2020) have also been proposed to counter this series of attack methods. Since CO problems can usually be encoded by a graph structure and inspired by (Dai et al., 2018), which develops an RL based attack policy towards GNNs, we propose a novel and flexible attack and defense framework for CO solvers using both heuristic and RL methods.
Note that the recent adversarial graph matching (GM) network show how to fulfill attack or defense via perturbing or regularizing geometry property on the GM solver. (Zhang et al., 2020) degrades the

## 3 COMBINATORIAL OPTIMIZATION WITH ATTACK AND DEFENSE

## 3.1 PROBLEM FORMULATION

In general, a traditional CO problem _Q_ defined on graph _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) can be formulated as:

where **x** denotes the decision variable, _f_ ( **x** _|G_ ) represents the target function w.r.t. the specific CO problem and _hi_ ( **x** _, G_ ) denotes the set of constraints (usually encoded in graphs). However, due to the NP-hard nature (which is often the case in CO), it can be infeasible to find the optimal solution within polynomial time. Therefore, we denote a different solver _S_ (which gives the feasible solution _f_ ( _S_ ( _Q_ ) _|G_ )) to approach the global optimum _f[∗]_ ( _Q_ ).

It is worth noting that the optimum _f[∗]_ ( _Q_ ) of Eq. 1 will become no worse if we loosen part of the constraints _hi_ since the previous decision variable **x** is still feasible under the new setting. Intuitively, we may expect the solver to give a better (at least the same) solution on the new problem _Q[′]_ . However, we will show in this paper that many solvers are vulnerable to such perturbations and their solutions can become worse under our attacks, despite the loose bound _f[∗]_ ( _Q[′]_ ) _≤ f[∗]_ ( _Q_ ).
Given a solver _S_ and an original problem _Q_ represented by a graph _G_ , the adversarial attacker _g_ is asked to modify the graph _G_ into _G[′]_ to attack the solver _S_ , such that:

Here _T_ ( _·, ·_ ) _→{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ is an equivalency indicator (Dai et al., 2018) that tells whether two graphs _G_ and _G[′]_ satisfy a specified constraint. In short, the above equation tells that the attacker is aiming at making small modifications to the original graph, loosening the constraints while making the solver solution as bad as possible.
In this paper, concretely our attacker _g_ is allowed to modify edges (e.g. adding or removing edges) from _G_ to construct the new graph. Accordingly, we define the equivalency indicator as:

which ensures that the attacker can modify no more than _K_ edges of the original graph.

On the other hand, it is imperative to develop defense mechanism for against the above attacks. Notice that the attack methods we mentioned before have some degree of symmetry (adding/deleting edges), we can simply do reverse operations for defense. For example, if we can relax the constraints by removing edges while worsening the solver’s solution, then we can add some edges (constraints) and get a better solution (that is, the symmetry). In this paper, we focus on black-box attack and defense, which means we have no idea on the solver. This setting is practical especially considering there are plenty of commercial solvers e.g. Gorubi and CPLEX etc. We leave white box attack and defense for future work.

3.2 ATTACK VIA GRAPH MODIFICATION We devise both reinforce learning (RL) and heuristic based attackers. For RL, the popular Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017) framework is adopted. We also design three traditional heuristic attackers: random sampling, optimum-guided search and simulated annealing.

## 3.2.1 REINFORCE LEARNING BASED ATTACK

Eq. 2 is treated as the learning objective and we resort to reinforcement learning (RL) to optimize _G[′]_ in a data-driven manner. In general, we modify the graph structure and compute _f_ ( _S_ ( _Q[′]_ ) _|G[′]_ ) alternatively, getting rewards that will be fed into the PPO framework and train the agent iteratively.
**MDP Formulation.** Given an instance ( _S, G_ ), with a total modification budget, we model the attack via sequential edge modification as a Finite Horizon Markov Decision Process (MDP).

_•_ State. The current graph _G[k]_ (i.e. the graph _G[′]_ after _k_ actions) is treated as the state, whose nodes and edges encode both current input and constraints. The original graph _G_[0] is the starting state.

_•_ Action. As mentioned in Sec. 3.1, the attacker is allowed to add/delete edges in the graph. So a single action at time step _k_ is _a[k] ∈A[k] ⊆ E[k]_ . Here our action space _A[k]_ is usually a subset of all the edges _E[k]_ because we restrict the action space (i.e. abandon some useless edge candidates) according to the previous solution _S_ ( _Q[k]_ ) to speed up our algorithm. Furthermore, we decompose the action space ( _O_ ( _|V |_[2] ) _→ O_ ( _|V |_ )) by transforming the edge selection into two node selections: first selecting the starting node, then the ending node.
_•_ Reward. The new graph _G[k]_[+1] results in a new CO problem _Q[k]_[+1] whose objective becomes _f_ ( _S_ ( _Q[k]_[+1] ) _|G[k]_[+1] ). The reward is the increase of the objective:

**Random sampling.** In each iteration, an edge is randomly chosen to be modified in the graph and it repeats for _K_ iterations. We run _N_ attack trials and choose the best solution. It can reflect the robustness of solvers with the cost of time complexity _O_ ( _NK_ ).

**Optimum-guided search (OG-Search).** It focuses on finding the optimum solution during each iteration. We use beam search to maintain the best _B_ current states and randomly sample _M_ different actions from the candidates to generate next states. The number of iterations is set to be no more than _K_ . Its time complexity is _O_ ( _BMK_ ).
**Simulated Annealing (SA).** Simulated annealing (Van Laarhoven & Aarts, 1987) comes from the idea of annealing and cool-

ing used in physics for particle crystallization. In our scenario, a higher temperature indicates a higher probability of accepting a worse solution, allowing to jump out of the local optimum. As the action number increases and the temperature decreases, we will be more conservative and tend to reject the bad solution. The detailed process is shown in Appendix B and we will repeat the algorithm for _N_ times. SA is a fine-tuned algorithm and we can use grid search to find the best parameter to fit the training set. ## 4 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

We conduct experiments on three representative tasks: Directed Acyclic Graph Scheduling, Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem and Fraud Coverage. The former two problems are popular problems in CO. The third problem is originated from a real-world transaction dataset. The detailed graph embedding for the three tasks is shown in Appendix C. In Appendix G, we provide the training and evaluation parameters of different solvers for fair time comparison and reproducibility. All experiments are run on RTX 2080Ti and RTX 3090 (see Appendix H for the detailed testbed).

## 4.1 TASK I: DIRECTED ACYCLIC GRAPH SCHEDULING

Task scheduling for heterogeneous systems and various jobs is a popular problem due to its practical importance. Many systems formulate the job stages and their dependencies as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) (Saha et al., 2015; Chambers et al., 2010; Zaharia et al., 2012). The data center has limited computing resources to allocate the jobs with different resource requirements. These jobs can run in parallel if all their parent jobs have finished and the required resources are available. Our goal is to **minimize the finish time of the jobs i.e. we should finish all jobs as soon as possible.**

**Solvers.** We choose three popular heuristic solvers as our attack targets. First, the Shortest Job First algorithm chooses the jobs greedily with minimum completion time. Second, the Critical Path algorithm analyzes the bottleneck and finishes the jobs in the critical path sequence. Third, the Tetris (Grandl et al., 2014) scheduling algorithm models the jobs as 2-dimension blocks in the Tetris games according to their finish time and resource requirement.

**Attack model.** The edges in a DAG represent job dependencies, and removing edges will relax the constraints. After removing existing edges in a DAG, it is obvious that the new solution will be equal or better than the original one since there are less restrictions. As a result, in the DAG scheduling tasks, the attack model is to selectively remove existing edges.

**Defense model.** We propose to add non-existing edges on the input graph associated with the CO problem, and obviously the new solution under more constraints is still feasible for the original CO problem. The motivation is to help tune the graph structure to be more suitable for heuristic

algorithms. To reduce the action space, we propose to pre-process the node pairs that already have dependencies and remove the corresponding edges in the candidate set.

**Dataset.** We use the TPC-H dataset (http://tpc.org/tpch/default5.asp), which is composed of business-oriented queries and concurrent data modification. Many DAGs have tens or even hundreds of stages with different duration and numbers of parallel tasks. As each DAG in TPC-H dataset represents a computation job, we gather the DAGs randomly and generate three different datasets, TPC-H-50, TPC-H-100, TPC-H-150, of each containing 50 training and 10 testing samples. Each DAG node has two properties: execution time and resource requirement.

**Results for attack.** Table 3 reports the results of our four attack methods, where RL outperforms other learning-free methods in most cases, illustrating the correctness of our feature extraction techniques and training framework. It is worth noting that even the simplest random attack can cause a significant performance degradation to the CO solvers, showing their vulnerability and the effectiveness of the attack framework.

**Results for attack and defense.** Table 4 and Fig. 3 show the results of attack and defense experiments on DAG. In general, the defense model can compensate for the damage of the attack and can even obtain better solutions than the baseline in some cases. It’s also worth noting that for some instances, the edges removed in the attack stage will be added back in the defense.

4.2 TASK II: ASYMMETRIC TRAVELING SALESMAN PROBLEM The classic traveling salesman problem (TSP) is to **find the shortest cycle to travel across all the cities.** Here we tackle the even challenging asymmetric TSP (ATSP) for its generality.

**Solvers.** Four algorithms are treated as our attack targets: i) Nearest Neighbour greedily adds the nearest city to the tour. ii) Furthest Insertion finds the city with the furthest distance to the existing cities in the tour and inserts it. iii) Lin-Kernighan Heuristic (LKH3) (Helsgaun, 2017) is the traditional SOTA TSP solver. iv) Matrix Encoding Networks (MatNet) (Kwon et al., 2021) claims as a SOTA learning-based solver for ATSP and flexible flow shop (FFSP).

**Attack model.** The attack is to choose an edge and half its value, after which we will get a better theoretical optimum. To reduce the action space, we will not select the edges in the current path predicted by the solver at the last time step.

**Defense model.** First we calculate the optimal path by the solver and add these edges to the candidate set. The action is to modify an edge’s weight by doubling the distance of that edge in order to encourage the solver to explore other paths.

**Dataset.** It comes from (Kwon et al., 2021) consisting of ‘tmat’ class ATSP instances which have the triangle inequality and are widely studied by the operation research community (Cirasella et al., 2001). We solve the ATSP of three sizes, 20, 50 and 100 cities. The distance matrix is fully connected and asymmetric, and each dataset consists of 50 training samples and 20 testing samples.

**Results for attack.** Table 5 reports the attack results of four target solvers. In general, the learningbased solvers (e.g. MatNet) or those with intrinsic randomness (e.g. LKH3) show stronger robustness to the attacks. Furthermore, it is notable that the RL based attack outperforms in most cases.
**Results for attack and defense.** Table 6 shows that the defense model works well on ATSP. In addition to making up the degeneration by attack, in some cases it even obtains shorter total distance.

## 4.3 TASK III: FRAUD COVERAGE

Our last problem instance refers to Fraud Coverage (FC), which is an emerging NP-Complete (details in Appendix D.1) problem abstracted from real life: the growing online transactions have also spawned criminals and scams. The transactions _E_ can be classified into black (fraudulent) events _B_ and white (normal) events _W_ . To block fraud events, the bank system designs a series of rules _R_ to

The goal is to **select a subset of rules** _R ⊆R_ **to maximize the coverage of fraudulent monetary values while affecting no more than** _K_ **white events.** The problem can be represented by a bipartite graph, where any edge exists between a rule node and an event node only when the event is deemed as black by the rule. Formally:

where _w_ ( _·_ ) denotes the monetary value of a certain transaction event, _C_ ( _·_ ) denotes the set of events covered by a rule that are deemed as black events, and _C_[+] ( _·_ ) and _C[−]_ ( _·_ ) denotes the subset of events in _C_ ( _·_ ) with true labels being black and white, respectively.

**Solvers.** As an emerging real-world CO task, the FC problem is very challenging and here we propose three different solvers as the target for attacking. First, the trivial Local algorithm which iterates over the rules sequentially, adding any rules that will not exceed the threshold. Second, a more intelligent Greedy Average algorithm that always chooses the most cost-effective (the ratio of the increase of black event money values to the increase of number of covered white events) rule at each step until the constraint isn’t satisfied. Third, we formulate the problem into standard ILP form (details in Appendix D.2) and solve it by Gurobi.

**Attack model.** Intuitively, when a white event is mislabeled as a black event, the FC problem will achieve an equal or better optimum _f[∗]_ ( _Q[′]_ ), since we can possibly cover more white events while not exceeding the threshold. In our attack model, we focus on the attack toward the edges rather than the nodes. We choose to add non-existing black edges that connect rules to black events, which leads to a theoretically better optimum and can potentially mislead the solvers. Further, in order to reduce action space, we only select the unchosen rules, otherwise it will be useless since adding edges for selected rules would not affect a solver’s output solution. Here we report the attack method on adding black edges and present the results for attacking black nodes in the Appendix F.1.

**Defense model.** Similar to the attack method, as defense we remove the existing black edges that connect rules to black events. To reduce action space, we select the rules chosen in the prior solution, since deleting black edges for the unchosen rules will not change the solution.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND OUTLOOK

We have presented a general adversarial attack and defense framework called ROCO on top of combinatorial solvers. For attack, we devise both RL and traditional heuristic attackers to modify the underlying graph structure of combinatorial problems. Meanwhile, we propose a simple yet effective defense mechanism to modify the ill-posed problem in a reversed way to increase the robustness of combinatorial solvers. Experiments show the effectiveness of our paradigm and techniques.

The proposed paradigm opens up large space for further research, at least in the following aspects: 1) new attack/defense techniques beyond graph structure but also node/edge attribute; 2) iterative adversarial training for defense model, especially for learning-based solvers (at least in the sense of tailored data augmentation); 3) white-box attack/defense when the solver information is known.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a timely and significant research gap: the vulnerability of combinatorial optimization (CO) solvers—both heuristic and learning-based—to adversarial perturbations of input graph structures. The motivation is strong, as the ability to trigger performance degradation in critical optimization tasks (e.g., job scheduling, routing) via minor modifications is a grave security and robustness concern. However, the manuscript currently faces a clarity issue regarding the definition of the "adversarial" threat model. While the authors distinguish their work from traditional image-space perturbations, they must provide more formal rigor in defining what constitutes a "valid" attack. Specifically, the paper claims that an attack should ensure the optimal solution "must not become worse," which is a non-trivial constraint; the authors need to explicitly explain how this is technically verified or guaranteed within their optimization framework. Without a more precise mathematical formulation of the adversarial objective and the constraints under which the "graph structure" is modified, the distinction between a valid "adversarial attack" and mere stochastic performance noise remains somewhat blurred. Strengthening the link between the theoretical definition of these graph-based perturbations and the practical implementation in the ROCO framework is essential for the paper to move beyond an empirical observation and fulfill its potential as a foundational study.

However, the subscript notation Q[k] is defined in 3.2.1 as a graph structure resulting from k 'attack' actions. The defense reward is defined here as the difference in the opposite order. However, the variables $G^{(k)}$ and $G^{(k+1)}$ have different meanings in the attack vs. defense context. In the defense, $G$ is substituted for $G^{(k+1)}$ and the objective is compared against the original/previous graph. While likely intended to be the 'symmetric' inverse of the attack, the indexing is ambiguous and inconsistent with the attacker's MDP flow, potentially leading to incorrect sign logic during implementation of the PPO agent.

The defense reward as written implies that if the defender successfully improves the objective (makes it smaller), the reward (f_old - f_new) would be positive. However, the notation f(S(Q^{(k)})|G) refers to the solver's performance on the original graph G given the k-th modified graph Q^{(k)}. This indexing is confusing because Q^{(k)} is the modified graph, but the condition |G implies using the original graph. comments.quote: The motivation is to help tune the graph structure to be more suitable for heuristic algorithms... in some cases it even obtains shorter total distance.
comments.explanation: In section 3.1, the defense is constrained such that the feasible space of $G'$ is a subset of $G$, based on the assumption that adding constraints (edges) makes the solution no worse (or better). However, in DAG scheduling, adding edges increases constraints, which typically restricts the search space for a scheduler, often leading to *longer* (worse) finish times, not better ones. The authors argue the opposite for the defense phase without sufficient explanation as to how restricting job parallelism leads to a shorter finish time. This creates a logical tension between their goal and the nature of the constraint.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Fraud Coverage (FC) objective sign/improvement direction
comments.quote: The goal is to select a subset of rules $R \subseteq R$ to maximize the coverage of fraudulent monetary values while affecting no more than $K$ white events.
comments.explanation: The text states the objective is to *maximize* fraud coverage. However, in Table 8, the 'Ratio (%)' column for the 'Attack' mode shows large negative values (e.g., -85.61, -94.60). In the context of the paper, an attack is designed to deteriorate solution quality. If the goal is maximization, the attacker should *reduce* the objective. A negative ratio in this context implies the objective value dropped, but the use of the term 'Ratio' and the interpretation of results in the text suggests 'Ratio' means the percentage of change. If the metric is 'Fraud$', a negative ratio clearly indicates the solver's performance degraded. The confusion arises because the text uses 'Ratio' interchangeably for metrics where higher is better (DAG scheduling time, where 'smaller is better' is inverted to 'larger is better') and where 'Ratio' indicates relative difference, making the table headers and results difficult to interpret consistently across tasks.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Ratio' calculation definition
comments.quote: In particular, we present a simple yet effective defense strategy... The ratio here is the mean of ratios on all test instances computed by the solved FC monetary value w.r.t. baselines.
comments.explanation: The paper fails to provide a formal definition of 'Ratio'. For DAG scheduling, the goal is to *minimize* finish time, and the authors define Ratio as 'time improvement' where 'larger is better' (suggesting it measures the *attacker's* success). For FC, the goal is *maximization*, but the Ratio in Table 8 is positive for 'Defense' (improvement) and negative for 'Attack' (degradation), yet the text claims 'The larger the ratio, the better attack performance'. This is mathematically inconsistent: if a large positive ratio is better for an attack, but the results in Table 8 show negative values for attacks, there is a fundamental disconnect in how the ratio is calculated or signed for different tasks.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in reward function
comments.quote: The defense MDP formulation is just the same as Sec. 3.2.1 except that we set $r = f(S(Q^{(k)})|G) - f(S(Q^{(k+1)})|G)$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2.1, the attack reward is defined as $r = f(S(Q^{(k+1)})|G) - f(S(Q^{(k)})|G)$, which represents the increase in the objective (to maximize the deterioration). The defense reward is defined here as the difference in the opposite order. However, the variables $G^{(k)}$ and $G^{(k+1)}$ have different meanings in the attack vs. defense context. In the attack, $G^{(k)}$ is the state *before* the attacker modifies it, and $G^{(k+1)}$ is the state *after* the modification. In the defense, $G$ is substituted for $G^{(k+1)}$ and the objective is compared against the original/previous graph. While likely intended to be the 'symmetric' inverse of the attack, the indexing is ambiguous and inconsistent with the attacker's MDP flow, potentially leading to incorrect sign logic during implementation of the PPO agent.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in reward function for defense
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the authors state the defense reward as r = f(S(Q^{(k)})|G) - f(S(Q^{(k+1)})|G). However, in the MDP formulation for attacking (Section 3.2.1), the reward is defined as the *increase* of the objective (Eq 5). If the solver's objective is to be minimized (as in DAG scheduling, where the goal is to minimize finish time), 'worsening' the solution means increasing the objective value. The defense reward as written implies that if the defender successfully improves the objective (makes it smaller), the reward (f_old - f_new) would be positive. However, the notation f(S(Q^{(k)})|G) refers to the solver's performance on the original graph G given the k-th modified graph Q^{(k)}. This indexing is confusing because Q^{(k)} is the modified graph, but the condition |G implies using the original graph. This potentially contradicts the previous definition where the objective is evaluated on the modified graph Q^{(k+1)}. This ambiguity makes the defender's training objective unclear.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of 'Normal Solution' in Table 4
comments.quote: and the all the ratio is computed by the solved finish time w.r.t. Normal solution.
comments.explanation: Table 4 presents a 'Ratio ↓' column for defense results. The text states ratios are computed relative to the 'Normal solution'. It is unclear if this refers to the 'Baseline' from Table 3 (the unmodified graph) or the 'Attacked solution' (the solution of the graph after the attack). If the ratio is meant to show the effectiveness of the defense, it should likely be defined relative to the attacked solution to show recovery, but 'Normal' strongly implies the original, unperturbed baseline. This ambiguity makes the reported numerical improvements difficult to interpret.

comments.title: Contradictory description of Gurobi observation
comments.quote: The result is shown in Fig. 4, where Gurobi’s solution time after attack (defense) significantly increases (decreases).
comments.explanation: In the paragraph describing Figure 4, the authors state that the solution time 'after attack (defense)' increases (decreases). While it is logical that an attack (which might make the problem harder) would increase solution time and a defense might decrease it, this grouping is grammatically ambiguous. It implies 'after attack' refers to the increase and 'after defense' refers to the decrease. However, the subsequent sentence ('This inspires us to attack toward the solvers’ solution time') suggests that the *goal* of the attack is to increase the time. If the attack purpose is to deteriorate solution *quality* (objective value), the increase in time might be a side effect. The text conflates solution quality deterioration with time complexity, which are distinct performance metrics.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper tackles an interesting and timely problem—adversarial robustness in combinatorial optimization solvers—and proposes a unified framework (ROCO) for both attack and defense. The central idea of perturbing graph structures to degrade solver performance is conceptually appealing and practically relevant, and the effort to evaluate on multiple problem domains and solver types (including commercial solvers like Gurobi) is commendable. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that undermine its clarity and contribution. First, the framing and motivation are diffuse: the connection between "adversarial attack" and "natural problem instance variation" is conflated without clear resolution, and the claimed novelty (i.e., being the "pioneering work" on adversarial attacks for CO solvers) is stated too strongly without sufficient discussion of related work on robustness and uncertainty in CO. Second, the attack and defense mechanisms are presented at a high level, but crucial technical details—such as how the attacker ensures that the optimal solution "must not become worse" (this seems contradictory to the goal of degradation), the precise nature of the black-box assumption, and the algorithmic instantiation of the defender—remain vague, making it difficult to assess the soundness and reproducibility of the approach. Third, the paper lacks a clear theoretical or empirical analysis of why the proposed defense is effective or how it trades off robustness against solution quality. Clarity is further hampered by typographical errors (e.g., "Combnaotorial") and a somewhat disjointed narrative that jumps between attack, defense, and evaluation without a well-defined problem formulation. Before publication, the authors should more precisely define the threat model, formalize the optimization objectives for both attacker and defender, provide a rigorous comparison with baseline methods, and substantiate the claimed generality of their framework with more detailed experimental analysis.

comments.title: Claim that optimal solution 'must not become worse' after loosening constraints is misapplied
comments.quote: Our attacker limits the number of attacked edges in the graph and guarantees that the optimal solution must not become worse.
comments.explanation: The sentence appears in the description of ROCO in Table 1 and the introduction. The authors state that their attacker 'guarantees that the optimal solution must not become worse.' However, the attack is designed to make the solver's solution worse, not the optimal solution. Earlier in Section 3.1, the authors correctly note that if constraints are loosened (by removing edges), the true optimum f*(Q') ≤ f*(Q). The attacker's goal is to degrade the solver's output f(S(Q')|G') relative to f(S(Q)|G), while the optimal bound becomes no worse. The prose in Table 1 conflates 'optimal solution' with 'solver solution.' This is a subtle but important logical confusion: the attacker does not aim to make the true optimum worse (it cannot); it aims to make the solver's solution worse. A careful reader might mistakenly think the attacker's objective is to worsen the mathematical optimum, which is impossible under the given operation. The context resolves that the authors likely meant the solver's solution, but the phrasing as written is incorrect and could mislead.

comments.title: Overstated claim about pioneering work
comments.quote: this is the pioneering work that formally studies adversarial attacks on combinatorial solvers, though their vulnerability has been occasionally recognized by the community.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their work is the first to formally study adversarial attacks on combinatorial solvers. However, the related work section (Section 2) already mentions existing works on adversarial attacks on graph matching solvers (Zhang et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2021). While those works are specific to graph matching, they are still combinatorial solvers. The statement 'pioneering work that formally studies adversarial attacks on combinatorial solvers' may overstate the novelty, as prior work on adversarial perturbations for graph matching exists. The authors later argue those works are 'deliberately tailored to the specific problem and can hardly generalize' and are white-box, but the claim of being 'pioneering' is too broad. A more precise claim would be 'first to study black-box adversarial attacks on general CO solvers' or similar.

comments.title: OCR error: 'Comnbnaotorial' should be 'Combinatorial'
comments.quote: Ro** bust **C** ombnaotorial **O** ptimization (ROCO)
comments.explanation: The word 'Comnbnaotorial' appears to be a typographical error from OCR. The intended word is 'Combinatorial.' This is a minor issue since the acronym ROCO is correctly defined and the context makes the intended word clear. However, if the paper were to be published, this should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguity in defense reward formulation
comments.quote: The defense MDP formulation is just the same as Sec. 3.2.1 except that we set r = f(S(Q[k])|G) − f(S(Q[k+1])|G) and use the symmetric action of the attacker.
comments.explanation: The reward for the defender is defined as the improvement in the solver's solution on the original graph G, not on the modified graph. However, the description 'symmetric action of the attacker' is vague. The attacker adds/deletes edges to relax constraints; the defender presumably does the reverse (adds constraints to shrink the feasible space). But the connection between the reward definition and the action symmetry is not explicitly justified. It is not clear whether the defender always adds edges (opposite of attacker's deletion) or can also delete edges. The phrase 'symmetric action' is insufficiently specified for reproduction. A reader would need to know: does the defender always add edges? Does it only add edges that were originally present? How is the action space constrained? This underspecification could hinder reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing justification for why defense solution on G' is feasible for original G
comments.quote: The new solution under stronger constraints is surely feasible for the original graph (then we can use it in the original graph to get f(S(Q')|G)).
comments.explanation: The authors claim that if the defender adds edges (strengthens constraints), the solution to the modified problem Q' is feasible for the original problem Q. While this is true if the added edges are a superset of the original constraints, the paper does not specify that the defender only adds edges that are present in the original graph or are consistent with original feasibility. If the defender adds an edge that was not in the original graph (e.g., a new dependency), it might make the feasible space a subset, but the solution might not be feasible for the original problem because the constraint did not exist. The statement assumes the defender only adds constraints that were originally present, but this is not explicitly stated. This could be clarified by specifying that defense modifications are limited to adding edges that existed in the original graph G.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in Table 1: 'classifcation' and 'Perturb.'
comments.quote:
image
classifcation
pixels
_ϵ_-Perturb.
comments.explanation: The word 'classifcation' is a typo for 'classification.' Also 'Perturb.' is an abbreviation but is acceptable. This is a minor OCR artifact that does not affect understanding.

comments.title: Missing reward formula for attacker
comments.quote: The reward is the increase of the objective:
comments.explanation: The reward equation is stated in prose but the mathematical expression is missing, likely due to an OCR omission. Without the explicit formula, the definition is incomplete, though it can be inferred from context (the improvement in objective). This is a minor technical gap.

comments.title: Missing critic value prediction equation
comments.quote: fed into a network (e.g. ResNet block) for value prediction:
comments.explanation: Equation (7) for the critic's value prediction is referenced but not shown. The text ends with a colon and a blank line. This is likely an OCR error. While the general idea is clear, the missing equation hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous joint probability selection in beam search
comments.quote: Select (a_1 , a_2) with top-B probabilities;
comments.explanation: The algorithm does not specify how the joint probability of (a1, a2) is computed from P(a1) and P(a2|a1). It could be the product, the average, or another combination. This ambiguity affects reproducibility of the beam search procedure.

comments.title: Inconsistency between 'top-B rewards' and sorting by solution
comments.quote: only those actions with top- B rewards will be reserved for the next search step
comments.explanation: The prose says actions are kept based on top-B rewards, but Algorithm 1 sorts by the solver's objective f(S|G') (i.e., the solution value) and selects top-B accordingly. Since the reward is defined as the increase in objective, sorting by solution value is not equivalent to sorting by reward (unless the base objective is constant). This discrepancy undermines the description of the beam search.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between attack results for Critical Path (job=50)
comments.quote: Table 4: Attack ratio 9.13 for Critical Path, job#=50. Table 3: RL=9.26, SA=9.58, OG-Search=9.03, Random=8.13
comments.explanation: The attack ratio in Table 4 (9.13) does not match any of the attack method results reported in Table 3 for the same solver and job size. It is close to OG-Search (9.03) but not exact. Since the paper does not specify which attack method was used for Table 4, this inconsistency raises concerns about reproducibility and the validity of the comparison.

comments.title: Minor numerical discrepancy for Shortest Job First (job=50)
comments.quote: Table 4: Attack ratio 1.37. Table 3: RL ratio 1.41
comments.explanation: The attack ratio for Shortest Job First job=50 in Table 4 is 1.37, while the RL attack in Table 3 gives 1.41. The difference is small but unexplained. It could be due to different random seeds or a different attack method, but the paper does not clarify.

comments.title: Inconsistency between action-space description and DAG attack model
comments.quote: the attack model is to selectively remove existing edges
comments.explanation: The paper states that the attacker can add or delete edges (Sec 3.1, 'adding or removing edges'). For DAG scheduling, removing edges loosens constraints (makes the optimum no worse), which aligns with the attack objective of making the solver's solution worse while keeping the optimum non-worsening. However, the paper also mentions that the attacker can add edges. For a DAG, adding an edge would tighten constraints, potentially making the optimum worse, which contradicts the attacker's guarantee that the optimal solution does not become worse (Eq 2's constraint `f*(Q') <= f*(Q)'). While the authors limit DAG attack to 'selectively remove existing edges', they do not explain why adding edges is not used for DAG. This is a logical gap: the general attack formulation permits both add/delete, but in DAG only deletions are used, presumably to maintain the optimal bound. The paper should clarify that for DAG, only deletions satisfy the equivalency indicator's implicit requirement; otherwise, a reader might incorrectly assume the general attack model applies uniformly.

comments.title: Ambiguity in defender's reward definition for original graph evaluation
comments.explanation: The defender reward uses the solution value evaluated on the original graph G, not on the modified graph G'. This is crucial because the defender's objective (Eq 4) is to minimize `f(S(Q')|G)`, i.e., the solver's solution on the attacked graph evaluated under the original graph's constraints. However, in the MDP, the state is G^k (the current modified graph), and the solver S is run on Q^k (the problem defined on G^k), producing `f(S(Q^k)|G^k)`. The reward formula uses `f(S(Q^k)|G)`, but it is not explained how the solver's solution on G^k is evaluated under G's constraints. Is a solution from G^k always feasible for G? The paper earlier states that the defender ensures feasibility by tightening constraints (G' subset of G), but that applies to the final defended graph. During sequential modifications, intermediate graphs may not be strict subsets of G (especially if the defender can add edges not in G). The text does not specify whether `f(S(Q^k)|G)` means re-solving the original problem with constraints from G but with the current solution from G^k (which may be infeasible), or whether it means evaluating the solution from G^k after projecting onto G. This is underspecified and could mislead a reader implementing the method. It is likely that the solver is run on Q^k and then the resulting solution is checked for feasibility w.r.t. G, but the paper does not discuss infeasibility handling.

comments.title: Underspecified action space restriction for RL attacker
comments.quote: we restrict the action space (i.e. abandon some useless edge candidates) according to the previous solution S(Q^k) to speed up our algorithm
comments.explanation: The RL attacker restricts action space based on the previous solver solution, but the criterion for 'useless' is never defined. For DAG, the paper later says 'we will not select the edges in the current path predicted by the solver at the last time step' for ATSP, but no similar clarification is given for DAG or FC. The phrase 'abandon some useless edge candidates' is too vague for reproducibility. A reader does not know whether this means excluding edges that would create cycles in DAG, excluding edges that are already critical in the solution, or some other heuristic. This is especially problematic because the action space decomposition (from O(|V|^2) to O(|V|)) and the restriction are separate; the restriction could significantly affect results if it removes the attacker's most effective actions. The paper should specify the rule for each task or provide a general principle.

comments.title: Underspecified defense action space for DAG scheduling
comments.quote: To reduce the action space, we propose to pre-process the node pairs that already have dependencies and remove the corresponding edges in the candidate set.
comments.explanation: For DAG defense, the authors propose adding non-existing edges, but they pre-process to exclude node pairs that already have dependencies. This is reasonable to avoid over-constraining. However, it is ambiguous whether 'dependencies' means direct edges, indirect paths (transitive closure), or both. In a DAG, adding an edge that creates a transitive dependency already implied by existing paths would not change the partial order but would still add a redundant constraint. The phrase is ambiguous; a careful reader might implement either only direct-edge exclusion or all reachable pairs. The latter would be more computationally expensive. The paper should clarify this choice, as it affects the size of the action space and the defender's effectiveness.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in ATSP attack model: halving edge weight vs. adding edges
comments.quote: The attack is to choose an edge and half its value
comments.explanation: The original attack formulation (Eq 3) only allows adding/removing edges, not modifying edge weights. For ATSP, the attacker halves an edge's weight, which is a weighted-graph modification not captured by the symmetric difference budget on edge existence. The equivalency indicator T(G,G') = 1 iff |E Δ E'| ≤ K does not apply to weight changes; it would treat the edge as still present. The paper does not explain how weight halving is accommodated by the theoretical framework. This is a significant gap: the attack mechanism for ATSP is fundamentally different from the one defined in Problem Formulation (edge addition/removal). Either the formalism should be extended to allow weight modifications, or the ATSP attack should be described within a broader interpretation (e.g., viewing each weight as a separate edge type). The paper glosses over this inconsistency, which undermines the claim that the attack model is unified across tasks.

comments.title: Overclaim about defense improving solutions for normal (unattacked) instances
comments.quote: the defense RL agent can not only play a defensive role against the attacked problem instance, but can also help further improve the solution of normal instances
comments.explanation: The paper claims the defense can improve solutions on normal instances, and Table 4 shows some cases where defense yields lower finish time than 'Normal' (e.g., Shortest Job First at job#=100: Normal 38.3202, Defense 38.0470, ratio -0.71% lower is better). However, the mechanism is unclear: the defender adds constraints, which should make the feasible set smaller, so the optimal solution cannot improve (only worsen or stay same). The only way the solver's solution could improve is if the original solver was failing to find the true optimum, and the additional constraints guide it to a better solution by changing the heuristic search landscape. The paper vaguely mentions 'help tune the graph structure to be more suitable for heuristic algorithms' but does not explain or justify this with theory or ablation. Claiming that defense 'can also help further improve the solution of normal instances' is an overstatement without explanation of when and why this occurs. The phrase 'as will be shown in some of our experiments' is anticipatory, but the results (Table 4) do not provide statistical significance or analysis of when improvement occurs. This could mislead readers into thinking the defender is universally beneficial even without attacks.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: objective function notation in attacker reward
comments.explanation: In the paper summary, the attacker reward is defined as the increase in objective. For minimization problems (DAG scheduling minimizes finish time, ATSP minimizes tour length), the attacker wants to maximize the objective (make it worse). Using r = f(...|G^{k+1}) - f(...|G^k) means a positive reward corresponds to an increase in objective, which aligns with the attacker's goal for minimization tasks (make the time longer). However, for a maximization problem (Fraud Coverage might be maximizing coverage?), the attacker would want to decrease the objective. The paper does not specify whether the reward sign is adjusted per task or if all tasks are minimization. The summary says 'increase of objective' generically, but the attacker's objective Eq 2 is to maximize f(S(Q')|G'), which is increasing the objective regardless of whether it's cost or reward. This is a minor ambiguity: a reader working with a maximization task would need to invert the reward sign. The paper should clarify that the reward is always defined as the change in the solver's objective, and the attacker aims to maximize it, so for minimization tasks the attacker increases the objective (worsens solution), while for maximization tasks the attacker decreases the objective. The current text could mislead if not interpreted with context.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between Table 7 and Table 8 for FC Gurobi attack results
comments.quote: Table 7: Gurobi(1s) 30-3K: RL attack ratio **50.68** (no std). Table 8: Gurobi attack Fraud$=2.7194, ratio=-85.61%.
comments.explanation: The ratio in Table 7 is defined as the solved FC monetary value relative to baseline (so expected value ≈ (attacked value)/(baseline)*100). For Gurobi at 30-3K, baseline=18.8934, Table 8 attacked value=2.7194 gives a ratio of about 14.4%, not 50.68%. The discrepancy persists for all three problem sizes: at 60-6K, Table 7 RL=100.00 (implying no change), but Table 8 shows a 94.60% drop; at 100-10K, Table 7 RL=92.01 while Table 8 shows a 90.53% drop (attacked value ≈9.5% of normal, not 92%). This indicates a fundamental arithmetic error in reporting or a mislabeling of what the ratio represents. Given that the authors claim the attack is highly effective against Gurobi, the numbers in Table 8 seem more plausible, but the inconsistency undermines confidence in both tables.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Furthest Insertion baseline for ATSP-100 between the two tables
comments.quote: Table 5 (ATSP attack results): Furthest Insertion | 100 | 1.9209. Table 6 (ATSP defense results): Furthest Insertion | Normal | 1.9272 (same column ATSP-100).
comments.explanation: The baseline (normal) distance for the Furthest Insertion solver on ATSP-100 should be the same in both tables. Table 5 reports 1.9209, while Table 6 reports 1.9272. The difference is about 0.3%, which is small but suggests either a rounding error, a different random seed, or a mis-transcribed number. For the other solvers (Nearest Neighbour, LKH3, MatNet) the values match exactly between the two tables, so this outlier is likely an error.

comments.title: Missing standard deviations for RL attack results in Tables 5 and 7
comments.quote: Table 5: RL column entries have no ± std (e.g., **12.94**, **10.26**, etc.). Table 7: Gurobi RL entries have no ± std (e.g., **50.68**, **100.00**, **92.01**).
comments.explanation: The other attack methods (Random, OG-Search, SA) are reported with mean ± std over 10 trials. The RL results are given as single numbers without any variability measure. The paper states 'Since the former three algorithms are inherently stochastic, we will run them multiple times to calculate the mean and standard deviation for fair comparison.' RL training also involves stochasticity (action sampling, beam search), so omitting uncertainty for RL makes the comparison incomplete and less rigorous. This is a reporting gap.

comments.title: Missing x-axis label in Figure 4
comments.quote: Mode Normal 8 Attack Defense 6 4 2 0 30 60 100 number of rules Gurobi solution time(s)
comments.explanation: The figure text shows 'number of rules' on the x-axis, but the x-axis labels are 30, 60, 100. The summary mentions FC problems where the number of rules is a parameter. However, the figure itself appears to have a missing or garbled axis label (the text says '30 60 100' but the axis label says 'number of rules' which is correct). The real issue is that the figure caption says 'Gurobi solution time(s)' but the y-axis label is not shown in the OCR text. This is a minor formatting/OCR issue that does not affect understanding.

comments.title: Unclear statement about Gurobi optimality in passage vs. summary
comments.quote: Besides, as a commercial solver, Gurobi should be able to obtain optimal solutions if in sufficient time (assuming we have unlimited computational resources). So we record the time for Gurobi to find the optimal solution under attack and defense.
comments.explanation: The passage claims Gurobi 'should be able to obtain optimal solutions if in sufficient time', which is consistent with the summary's description of ILP solved by Gurobi. However, the FC problem is NP-Complete (per summary), so for large instances Gurobi might not find the optimal solution in any feasible time. The phrase 'if in sufficient time (assuming we have unlimited computational resources)' is a reasonable caveat that acknowledges this. No real issue here; the authors are appropriately cautious.

comments.title: Gurobi solution time bar chart shows 'Attack' time lower than 'Normal' but text says opposite
comments.quote: The result is shown in Fig. 4, where Gurobi’s solution time after attack (defense) significantly increases (decreases).
comments.explanation: The text says 'Gurobi’s solution time after attack (defense) significantly increases (decreases)'. Looking at the figure text, the bars for 'Attack' appear around 8 seconds while 'Normal' bars are around 2-4 seconds, and 'Defense' bars are around 0-2 seconds. This is consistent: attack increases time, defense decreases time. However, the figure OCR shows garbled data ('Mode Normal 8 Attack Defense 6 4 2 0...'), which is confusing. The intended meaning is clear from context: attack makes Gurobi take longer to find optimal, defense speeds it up. No real inconsistency, just poor OCR rendering of the figure.

comments.title: Figure 4 x-axis inconsistency: 'number of rules' but values are 30, 60, 100
comments.quote: Mode Normal 8 Attack Defense 6 4 2 0 30 60 100 number of rules Gurobi solution time(s)
comments.explanation: The x-axis labels are 30, 60, 100, which likely refer to the number of rules in the FC problem (a parameter of the problem instances). The summary says experiments are conducted on '3 datasets (20 instances) of different sizes'. This matches: 30, 60, 100 rules. The axis label 'number of rules' is correct. No issue.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in symmetry between attack and defense for FC
comments.quote: For attack, we devise both RL and traditional heuristic attackers to modify the underlying graph structure of combinatorial problems. Meanwhile, we propose a simple yet effective defense mechanism to modify the ill-posed problem in a reversed way
comments.explanation: The summary states: 'FC attack model: add non-existing black edges... FC defense model: remove existing black edges...'. This is symmetric (add vs. remove). However, the passage says defense modifies 'in a reversed way' which is consistent. The defense action space is restricted to 'rules chosen in prior solution' while attack is restricted to 'unchosen rules'. This asymmetry is acknowledged in the summary and is a task-specific design choice. No issue here.
